title: Chapter 29: The Investigation
content: Three days after the fire, my cottage smells like someone else's disaster.

The scent came in on the investigators' clothes — burnt electronics and chemical smoke, that acrid sweetness of melted plastic and vaporized solder. Every time Agent Reyes shifts in her chair, the smell freshens, like she carried the Cliff House out of the ashes and into my living room on the soles of her shoes.
I wrap both hands around my coffee mug. It's gone cold. Has been for an hour. I don't drink it.
"Ms. Chen." Agent Reyes flips a page in her folder. She doesn't look up. "Walk me through the evening of June eleventh one more time."

I've told her three times. She knows I've told her three times. This is the test — not the answer, but the consistency. Whether my story hold together under repetition the way truth does, or whether it frays the way lies do.
"I was at my cottage," I say. "I left the Cliff House around nine PM. I came home. I went to bed. I woke up to a text from Tom Hargrove saying there was a fire."

Reyes looks up. Her eyes are dark brown, almost black, and they don't blink often enough. She's maybe forty, with short-cropped hair and a silver watch that catches the morning light every time she moves her hand. The watch is the only thing about her that's decorative. Everything else — the navy blazer, the pressed slacks, the recorder clicking softly on my kitchen table — is function.
"And Mr. Hargrove sent this text at what time?"
"Four seventeen AM."
"You remember the exact time."
"I looked at my phone. That's what I saw."

The recorder clicks. Reyes pressed a button — pause, I think, or mark. A tiny metal sound in the quiet of my cottage. Outside, a scrub jay screams. Inside, there's just the hum of my refrigerator and the low static of professional patience.
"You were making a documentary," Reyes says. It's not a question.
"That's right. About Phantom Ledger. About Vivienne Sterling."
"How long were you embedded at the Cliff House?"
"I wasn't embedded. I was a guest. Vivienne gave me access to film the parties, conduct interviews, observe the company culture."
"Four months," Reyes says. "Almost every day."
"I was thorough."
She closes the folder. Opens a different one. The paper is crisp — fresh from a printer, the ink still sharp. She doesn't show me what's inside.
"Tell me about the parties, Ms. Chen."

I take a breath. The cold coffee bitterness coats my tongue. "Vivienne hosted gatherings. Tech industry people, investors, artists. They were networking events. Lavish. The house was designed for spectacle."
"What kind of spectacle?"

"Art installations. Holographic displays. One night she had bioluminescent jellyfish projected into the pool. Another time, a drone swarm that spelled out the Phantom Ledger logo over the ocean." I pause. "It was aesthetic. That was the point."
"The point," Reyes repeats. "And what was the point of the algorithm?"
I set the mug down. "I'm not a techie, Agent. I couldn't explain the algorithm to you if I tried."
"Humor me."
"Vivienne described it as a predictive trading system. It identified patterns in market behavior and executed trades ahead of the curve. That's what she told the investors. That's what I filmed."
"And did you believe her?"
"I believed she believed it. Whether it was real — that wasn't my question. My question was why people wanted to believe in it so badly that they'd hand over their money without proof."
Reyes almost smiles. Almost. The corner of her mouth twitches, then resets.
"Ms. Chen. You were at that house almost every day for four months. You expect me to believe you didn't know Vivienne Sterling?"
The paper in her folder rustles. I can hear it — the dry whisper of official documents, the weight of a case being built.
"I knew the myth," I say. "Nobody knew the person."
It's the truest thing I've said all morning. And the most useless to her.

Reyes stands. She walks to my window, hands clasped behind her back, studying the view of the Pacific like she's memorizing exit routes. The jay has stopped screaming. The cottage is so quiet I can hear my own pulse in my ears.

"We found no body," she says, still facing the window. "Melted laptop. Some damaged artwork. Fire damage consistent with an accelerant, but not an explosive device. It was controlled. Small. Deliberate."
"I wouldn't know."
She turns. "You wouldn't know." She says it the way a surgeon says interesting while looking at an X-ray. Not believing. Not disbelieving. Filing.
She returns to the table. Sits. Pulls something from the folder and slides it across the table toward me.

A printout. Several pages, stapled at the corner. The paper is warm from the folder, or maybe from her hands. I pick it up and feel the slight texture of laser-printed pages — smooth but not slick, with that faint grain that catches the light when you angle it right.

Blockchain data. Wallet addresses. Transaction hashes. Timestamps. Most of it means nothing to me — strings of alphanumeric characters, rows of numbers. But the arrows are clear. Flow diagrams. Money moving from one point to another, to another, to another.

"We've traced three hundred and forty million dollars from Phantom Ledger's primary wallet through a series of proxy wallets," Reyes says. Her voice is calm. Methodical. Like she's reading a weather report instead of describing the largest financial disappearance in the bay area this decade. "The trail goes cold at a decentralized exchange in the Marshall Islands. But this isn't over. Digital money isn't invisible — it's just very well hidden."
I look at the printout. My thumb traces one of the arrows. A proxy wallet. Then another. Then another. Chaining, splitting, recombining. Like a river delta. Like veins.
Jax.

The pattern has her fingerprints all over it — not literal, but structural. The way the wallets nest inside each other, the timing intervals, the use of liquidity pools to obscure the trail. It's elegant. It's mathematical. It's the kind of architecture that only someone who thinks in systems would design.
But Reyes is right. And the realization drops through me like a stone into black water.

Blockchain is public by nature. Every transaction, every wallet, every hash — it's all recorded. The ledger doesn't forget. Jax's glitch was brilliant, layering proxies and routing through decentralized exchanges where KYC requirements are a joke, scattering the assets across jurisdictions that don't cooperate with U.S. investigators. She bought time. She bought confusion. She bought the fog that makes three hundred and forty million dollars look like it simply ceased to exist.
But brilliant is not the same as invisible.

The proxies are real addresses with real transaction histories. The exchange in the Marshall Islands has a server somewhere, logs somewhere, a compliance officer somewhere who might talk if the right pressure was applied. The assets didn't vanish — they moved. And movement leaves tracks.
I set the printout down. My fingers feel stiff. The paper grain is still there under my thumb.
"That's a lot of numbers," I say.
Reyes watches me. That too-still gaze. "It is."
"I don't know what any of it means."
"Of course not." She takes the printout back, slides it into the folder, closes the folder. The sound is quiet and final. "But someone does."
She doesn't say who. She doesn't have to.
The recorder clicks. The interview is over.
Reyes stands. She hands me a card — white, plain, government issue. Agent Diana Reyes, FBI Financial Crimes Unit. A phone number.
"If you think of anything," she says.
"Of course."
She lets herself out. I don't move. I sit at my kitchen table with the cold coffee and the ghost-smell of burnt electronics and the click of the recorder still echoing in the wood of the table.
The jay starts screaming again.
Jordan Keeler cuts a deal with the DOJ two days later.
I hear about it from Tom, who calls me on a morning when the fog hasn't lifted and the Pacific is just a sound — a low, rhythmic churn somewhere beyond the grey.
"Full cooperation," Tom says. His voice is flat. Tired. "Immunity in exchange for testimony."
My hand tightens on the phone. "About what?"
"Everything. The surveillance device. Jax's counter-intelligence operation. The short position they took against Phantom Ledger." A pause. "Jordan gave them the whole playbook."
I close my eyes. I can see Jordan — their sharp face, their restless hands, the way they always looked like they were calculating three moves ahead. Which, it turns out, they were.
"They're saving themselves," I say.
"Wouldn't you?"

The question sits between us like a third person on the line. I think about the cottage, the recorder, Reyes's dark eyes. I think about the blockchain printout, the arrows, the proxy wallets disappearing into the Pacific of the financial system.
"Yeah," I say. "I would."

I don't blame Jordan. That's the thing. I can't. They were caught between a company built on fraud and a federal investigation closing in like a jaw. They did what anyone with survival instincts would do: they traded what they knew for the right to keep their life. The surveillance device — the one Jax found, the one that gave Vivienne her leverage over Jordan — becomes evidence. The short position becomes proof of consciousness of guilt. And Jax's counter-intel, the elegant trap she set to turn Vivienne's own surveillance against her — Jordan lays it all out for the government like a blueprint.
Which means the government now knows what Jax can do. What she did. The kind of mind they're looking for.
The ticking starts. Soft, inside my chest. Like a clock I can't stop.
That night, the cottage is too quiet.

No parties. No holographic whales. No drone swarms spelling company logos across the sky. No Vivienne — laughing, commanding, filling every room she enters with the particular gravity of someone who believes the world orbits around her. No Jax — quiet, brilliant, looking at me like I'm the one mystery she hasn't solved yet.
Just me. The desk lamp. The hum of the refrigerator. The distant churn of the ocean.

I open my laptop. The screen glows blue-white, casting my shadow against the wall. I navigate to the folder I've been adding to for months — documents, interview transcripts, notes, outlines. A documentary that will never be finished because the ending turned out to be something no camera could capture.

But there's another file. A manuscript. I started it weeks ago, in the margins of the project, in the hours between filming and sleeping. It grew the way kelp grows — silently, persistently, tangling around everything.
Gilded Glitch.

I open it. The cursor blinks at the top of the page. The title sits there, simple and strange, a name I chose because it felt true. Gilded: the gold paint on everything Phantom Ledger touched — the parties, the promises, the myth of Vivienne Sterling. Glitch: the crack in the code, the moment the system failed the way systems do, beautifully and catastrophically.
I scroll to the beginning. The first line.
*The holographic whales came at 2 AM, and everything that followed was a signal.*

I read it twice. I remember the night — the blue light on Vivienne's face, the massive projected shapes rolling through the Cliff House living room, the way everyone went silent and then erupted in applause. I remember Jax standing in the back, not clapping. Watching. Her eyes tracking the light the way a navigator tracks stars.
She was already reading the signal. She was already building the glitch.

The manuscript is three hundred pages. It has the parties, the algorithm, the investors, the surveillance, the fire, the disappearance. It has everything. Which means it has too much. If Agent Reyes got her hands on this document, she wouldn't need Jordan Keeler's testimony. She'd have motive, method, and opportunity laid out in narrative form, annotated and cross-referenced.
Evidence. Or exoneration. Depending on who reads it.
I close the laptop. The screen goes dark. The cottage settles back into silence.
But something is still blinking.

Not on the screen. Not on the wall. In the logic of the thing. In the way blockchain ledgers work — every transaction recorded forever, immutable, public, waiting for someone patient enough and methodical enough to read the pattern through the noise.

Agent Reyes has the blockchain. Jordan gave her the blueprint. And somewhere in the world — some city without an extradition treaty, some island with no questions asked, some house that isn't a glass castle on a cliff — Jax and Vivienne think they're safe.
They aren't.
The signal is still blinking. And Reyes knows how to follow the light.
